content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem. The difference with the easy version is highlighted in bold.**

You are given a tree containing NNN vertices and N−1N-1N−1 undirected edges. You have to color each vertex of the tree either red, blue, or green.

Each vertex must receive exactly one color, and there should also be at least one vertex having each color in the end.

For a vertex uuu, define score(u)\text{score}(u)score(u) to be the sum of distances from uuu, to the closest vertices of the other two colors.

More clearly, if uuu is colored red, let d1d_1d1​ be the minimum distance from uuu to a green vertex, and d2d_2d2​ be the minimum distance from uuu to a blue vertex.
Then, we have

score(u)=d1+d2\text{score}(u) = d_1 + d_2score(u)=d1​+d2​The distance between two vertices uuu and vvv is equal to the number of edges on the simple path between uuu and vvv in the tree.

The beauty of the tree is calculated as the sum of score(u)\text{score}(u)score(u) across all vertices uuu.

**Let MMM denote the minimum possible beauty of the tree across all valid colorings.

Count the number of valid colorings that attain a beauty of MMM.

This value can be large, so compute it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.**

**Two colorings are considered different if there exists at least one vertex with different colors in the two colorings.**


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of vertices in the tree.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N−1N-1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an undirected edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.




Output Format
For each test case you have to output a single integer: the **number** of valid colorings such that the **minimum beauty** of the tree can be achieved, modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The input edges describe a tree.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are 666 ways to obtain a valid coloring, since we assign three distinct colors to three vertices.
All of these colorings have a beauty of 888.
So, the minimum beauty is 888, and there are 666 colorings that attain this minimum.
Test case 222: One optimal coloring is to color vertex 333 red, vertex 222 blue, and vertices 1,4,51, 4, 51,4,5 all green.
The scores of the vertices under this coloring are [3,2,2,3,3][3, 2, 2, 3, 3][3,2,2,3,3], with the overall beauty coming out to be 131313.
It can be proved that there are 181818 possible different valid colorings that attain minimum beauty.
